paragraphs.text: First, I was under the impression that Paul -- Thomas Massie was part of this amendment, which is somebody who just lost a race, very much blamed AIPAC, blamed groups lobbying on behalf of the -- of Israel.
paragraphs.cn: Pertama, saya pikir Paul -- Thomas Massie adalah bagian dari amandemen ini, dan dia baru saja kalah dalam pemilihan, sebagian besar karena AIPAC, karena kelompok yang melobi untuk Israel.

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: Tucker Carlson starting to do -- you know, is doing this --
paragraphs.cn: Tucker Carlson mulai -- Anda tahu, melakukan ini --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Tucker Carlson straight up left the party, right?
paragraphs.cn: Tucker Carlson langsung keluar, kan?

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: Exactly. Well, over -- and a big part of it is over Israel. So, I don't think this is just happening on the Democrat side. It's happening on both sides. But more on the Democrat side. And I also think, listen, it scares me, the anti-Semitic undertones that are happening throughout the country. Not whether they're Israel. It's just that the racism in general, I would call it. And so, it does make me nervous that it's happening.
paragraphs.cn: Benar. Yah, sebagian besar karena masalah Israel. Jadi, saya pikir ini tidak hanya terjadi di pihak Demokrat. Ada di kedua sisi, tetapi lebih parah di pihak Demokrat. Dan juga, saya pikir, dengar, arus bawah antisemitisme yang muncul di seluruh negeri membuat saya takut. Bukan terhadap Israel, tapi apa yang saya sebut rasisme umum. Jadi, terjadinya hal ini benar-benar membuat saya gelisah.

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: But I think that this is going back to something like when you had all the Republicans say, if we win, we're never giving money to Ukraine. I think there is a lot of people that are just -- don't want Americans involved in other wars.
paragraphs.cn: Tapi saya pikir ini kembali ke situasi serupa, seperti semua Republikan berkata, jika kita menang, kita tidak akan pernah memberi Ukraina uang. Saya pikir banyak orang hanya tidak ingin Amerika terlibat dalam perang lain.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. Well, I think that there is a way, though, of saying, pretty definitively, that after October 7th the conversation around Israel has changed tremendously. Just looking at Pew research surveys from March. Looking at the people who have somewhat unfavorable opinions of Israel. We wanted to look at it via age. And for people under the age of 50, on the Democratic side, we've heard this, 84 percent. On the Republican side, 57. OK. So, we're clearing the majority mark.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Namun, saya pikir ada cara untuk mengatakan dengan cukup jelas bahwa setelah 7 Oktober, diskusi seputar Israel telah berubah drastis. Lihatlah survei Pew Research bulan Maret. Lihatlah mereka yang memiliki pandangan relatif negatif terhadap Israel. Kami ingin melihat berdasarkan usia. Di antara Demokrat di bawah 50 tahun, kami mendengar angka ini 84%. Di antara Republikan 57%. Jadi, kami jelas melebihi mayoritas.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And then ages 50 plus, it's not that much better, OK? So, the vote -- these lawmakers are appealing to someone. And then there's American's views on Israel in general, in 2022, 55 percent were favorable, in 2026, 37 percent. And in that context, it makes more sense for me to have been reading a headline back in June, in "Axios," where Trump was mad at Netanyahu, and he's yelling at him about Lebanon. But he doesn't just say that. He says, "you're f-ing crazy" and everyone hates you.
paragraphs.cn: Kemudian kelompok usia di atas 50 tahun, keadaannya tidak lebih baik, kan? Jadi, para anggota parlemen ini melayani seseorang. Lihat lagi pandangan keseluruhan orang Amerika terhadap Israel, pada tahun 2022 55% memiliki pandangan positif, pada tahun 2026 (seharusnya 2024? sesuai teks asli) adalah 37%. Dalam konteks ini, saya lebih bisa memahami judul berita yang saya baca di Axios pada bulan Juni, bahwa Trump marah pada Netanyahu, berteriak padanya tentang Lebanon. Tapi dia tidak hanya mengatakan itu. Dia berkata: "Kamu benar-benar gila", semua orang membencimu.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And I thought that was a weird, tacit acknowledgment by the White House that the public sentiment is not in Netanyahu's favor.
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir ini adalah pengakuan default yang aneh dari Gedung Putih bahwa sentimen publik tidak mendukung Netanyahu.

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.text: Well, I mean, I think President Trump is also frustrated with Netanyahu for other reasons because he's trying to get this peace agreement. He sees Netanyahu's strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon as something that's, you know, helping to derail potentially a longer term agreement with Iran. So, there is a -- there's a few other things that are going on there.
paragraphs.cn: Yah, maksud saya, saya pikir Presiden Trump frustrasi dengan Netanyahu karena alasan lain, karena dia mencoba mencapai kesepakatan damai. Dia pikir serangan Netanyahu terhadap Hizbullah di Lebanon, Anda tahu, mungkin bisa membantu mengganggu kesepakatan jangka panjang dengan Iran. Jadi, ada beberapa hal lain yang terjadi di sini.

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.text: But I wanted to pick up on what Ashley was saying about, I think this broader sentiment there is within the Democratic Party going back to October 7th in the 2024 election. We saw this with vice president, former Vice President Kamala Harris, you know, playing out over Israel. But you also see, within the Republican Party, this taking place on Israel.
paragraphs.cn: Tapi saya ingin melanjutkan dari apa yang dikatakan Ashley, saya pikir sentimen yang lebih luas di dalam Partai Demokrat ini bisa ditelusuri kembali ke 7 Oktober dan pemilu 2024. Kita melihat Wakil Presiden, mantan Wakil Presiden Kamala Harris seperti ini dalam masalah Israel. Tapi Anda juga melihat, di dalam Partai Republik, hal yang sama terjadi dalam masalah Israel.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: But can I go back to my point there --
paragraphs.cn: Tapi bisakah saya kembali ke poin saya --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Because it's not -- I always joke that lawmakers are a lagging indicator. They're not at the forefront of what the public view is on Israel. They are followers. And so, are they looking at numbers and saying, wait a second, there's some vulnerability here. Maybe I should be speaking differently.
paragraphs.cn: Karena ini bukan -- Saya selalu bercanda bahwa pembuat undang-undang adalah indikator yang tertinggal. Mereka tidak berada di garis depan pandangan publik terhadap Israel. Mereka adalah pengikut. Jadi, apakah mereka melihat angka-angka dan berkata, tunggu, ada kerentanan di sini. Mungkin saya harus mengubah pernyataan saya.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: I can answer this. It matters in the base of the left of the Democrats and in the base right of the Republicans. It's a very, very divisive issue. And to my point, 400 congressional seats are safe, Democrat or Republican. So, you can only be beat on this issue in your primary. In the general election, Audie, I promise you, I'm making these ads. We don't mention either one of these to a swing voter because it's not their number one issue.
paragraphs.cn: Saya bisa menjawab pertanyaan itu. Ini penting di basis sayap kiri Demokrat dan basis sayap kanan Republik. Ini adalah masalah yang sangat, sangat memecah belah. Dan, seperti yang saya katakan, 400 kursi Kongres aman, baik untuk Demokrat maupun Republik. Jadi, Anda hanya akan dikalahkan dalam pemilihan pendahuluan karena isu ini. Dalam pemilihan umum, Audi, saya jamin, saat saya membuat iklan ini, kami tidak akan menyebutkan salah satu dari ini kepada pemilih yang goyah, karena ini bukan isu utama mereka.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: They care about the price of everything in their life and not making enough money and they can't get ahead. Not saying it's not an important issue, but it's super important.
paragraphs.cn: Mereka peduli dengan harga segala sesuatu dalam hidup, tidak mendapatkan cukup uang, tidak bisa maju. Bukan berarti ini tidak penting, tapi ini sangat penting.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. I'm not saying that either, but I was showing you a poll of American's views, not extremist horseshoe views. We are talking a majority now.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Saya juga tidak bermaksud seperti itu, tapi saya tunjukkan pada Anda jajak pendapat opini publik Amerika, bukan pandangan tapal kuda ekstremis. Yang kita bicarakan sekarang adalah mayoritas.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: But those numbers, if you looked at the base of the party, the folks who actually vote, it's even tremendously more.
paragraphs.cn: Tapi angka-angka ini, jika Anda melihat basis partai, mereka yang benar-benar memilih, bahkan lebih tinggi.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: That's always code, the folks who actually vote.
paragraphs.cn: Ini selalu merujuk pada mereka yang benar-benar memilih.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I feel like that's a way of saying, there's some people who are excited now who aren't going to show up later.
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir ini adalah cara untuk mengatakan bahwa beberapa orang yang sekarang bersemangat tidak akan muncul nanti.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: Y'all got to remember, it's about winning elections.
paragraphs.cn: Kalian harus ingat, kuncinya adalah memenangkan pemilu.

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: Elected officials have access to different information than the American people of why a safe Israel, a strong Israel, is very important for our national security.
paragraphs.cn: Pejabat terpilih lebih memahami daripada publik Amerika mengapa Israel yang aman, Israel yang kuat sangat penting bagi keamanan nasional kita.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: And are they conveying this well enough to the voters?
paragraphs.cn: Apakah mereka cukup menyampaikan hal ini kepada pemilih?

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: I don't think they can.
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir mereka tidak bisa.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Or are they leaving this to the world of internet online personalities who will speak about it in more striking ways?
paragraphs.cn: Atau, apakah mereka akan menyerahkan masalah ini kepada para influencer online untuk dibicarakan dengan cara yang lebih menarik?

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.text: They may be leaving it but -- they may be leaving it but I think that they'll take votes supporting Israel because how important -- they know how important it is. It's a hard (INAUDIBLE).
paragraphs.cn: Mereka mungkin menghindar, tapi -- mereka mungkin menghindar, tapi saya pikir mereka akan memilih mendukung Israel karena mereka tahu betapa pentingnya ini. Ini sulit (tidak jelas).

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Well, this is going to come up more. We're actually going to have a guest on who can talk about this and other things. We're going to talk about why the party is evolving one way or another. And we're going to have live here a Michigan Senate candidate, who's going to talk about the party's future. Plus, President Trump calling out Republicans. Can Congress function without his approval? Group chat is back after the break.
paragraphs.cn: Baiklah, masalah ini akan lebih sering disinggung. Kami sebenarnya akan mengundang seorang tamu untuk membahas topik ini dan hal-hal lainnya. Kami akan membahas mengapa partai ini berkembang dengan cara tertentu. Kami juga akan terhubung langsung dengan seorang kandidat senator dari Michigan yang akan berbicara tentang masa depan partai. Selain itu, Presiden Trump sedang mengkritik anggota Partai Republik. Tanpa dukungannya, bisakah Kongres berfungsi normal? Sesi obrolan grup akan kembali setelah iklan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, after recent primary victories by far-left, even Democratic socialist candidates in New York, the party is grappling with a surge that some have compared to the Tea Party movement Republicans faced 16 years ago. For some Democrats, that insurgent movement is not welcome.
paragraphs.cn: Setelah kandidat sayap kiri ekstrem bahkan demokrat sosialis memenangkan pemilihan pendahuluan baru-baru ini di New York, Partai Demokrat menghadapi gelombang yang oleh sebagian orang disamakan dengan gerakan Tea Party yang dihadapi Partai Republik 16 tahun lalu. Bagi sebagian Demokrat, gerakan pemberontakan ini tidak populer.

paragraphs.speaker: James Carville
paragraphs.text: I actually do think it's time for Democrats to talk the "s" word "schism." I really do. And everybody's always said, no, no, we -- we're coalition, we're big tent. And at some -- there's just some (EXPLETIVE DELETED) that I can't be in the same tent with.
paragraphs.cn: Saya benar-benar berpikir sekarang saatnya bagi Partai Demokrat untuk membicarakan kata 'perpecahan'. Saya benar-benar berpikir begitu. Dan semua orang selalu berkata, tidak, tidak, kami -- kami adalah koalisi, kami adalah tenda besar. Tapi pada titik tertentu -- ada beberapa (umpatan telah dihapus) yang membuat saya tidak bisa berada di tenda yang sama dengan mereka.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, joining me now to discuss, Dr. Abdul El-Sayed. He is running for the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate in Michigan. Thank you so much for being here. We had one of your opponents on last week, so I'm glad you could come in. First, can we talk about the "s" word, as Carville describes it? ABDUL EL-SAYED (D),
paragraphs.cn: Sekarang, bergabunglah dengan kami untuk berdiskusi adalah Dr. Abdul El-Sayed. Dia mencalonkan diri untuk nominasi Partai Demokrat untuk kursi Senat AS di Michigan. Terima kasih banyak telah datang. Minggu lalu kami mengundang salah satu lawan Anda, jadi saya senang Anda bisa datang. Pertama, bisakah kita bicara tentang kata 'perpecahan' yang disebutkan Carville? Abdul El-Sayed (Demokrat):

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan Senate Candidate
paragraphs.text: Yes. You know, it's funny, because isn't he the guy who said it's the economy, stupid. I think that's the "s" word we should be talking about. It is the economy. If you go to Michigan, if you go to New York, if you go anywhere in the country, people are frustrated because they can't afford the basic means of a dignified life.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Anda tahu, ini menarik, karena bukankah dia orang yang berkata 'Ini ekonomi, bodoh'? Saya pikir itulah kata 'perpecahan' yang seharusnya kita bicarakan. Ini ekonomi. Jika Anda pergi ke Michigan, jika Anda pergi ke New York, jika Anda pergi ke mana pun di Amerika, orang-orang merasa frustrasi karena mereka tidak mampu membeli kebutuhan dasar untuk hidup layak.

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan Senate Candidate
paragraphs.text: The fact that the Democrats have not delivered on those issues, the fact that people feel like both sides of our politics are not listening, that's why you're seeing a lot of these candidates, who are speaking differently about that economy, winning.
paragraphs.cn: Partai Demokrat belum menghasilkan hasil dalam masalah-masalah ini, dan orang-orang merasa kedua belah pihak politik tidak mendengarkan, itulah mengapa Anda melihat banyak kandidat yang berbicara tentang ekonomi dengan cara berbeda sedang menang.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Isn't his argument that it's not the economy part he disagrees with, it's everything else. It's the things we spoke about earlier, policy about Israel and how whether or not to support that government and its operations. Maybe it's around billionaire taxes. They have substantive questions and concerns about the Democratic socialist position.
paragraphs.cn: Bukankah argumennya bahwa dia tidak setuju dengan bagian ekonomi, tapi dengan yang lainnya? Adalah hal-hal yang kita bicarakan sebelumnya, tentang kebijakan Israel, dan apakah mendukung pemerintah itu dan tindakannya. Mungkin tentang pajak miliarder. Mereka memiliki masalah dan kekhawatiran substansial tentang posisi demokrasi sosial.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I think one of the frustrations -- and, look, the real red scare we should be talking about is that Donald Trump is still president. Donald Trump is still doing things like taking us to wars we shouldn't be fighting. But I do want to step back and -- we do this thing of dissecting our politics as if all the things are not related. That you can talk about foreign policy, but you don't have to relate that to the economy.
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir salah satu frustrasinya adalah -- dan, lihat, ketakutan merah yang seharusnya kita bicarakan adalah Donald Trump masih menjadi presiden. Donald Trump masih melakukan hal-hal seperti menyeret kita ke dalam perang yang seharusnya tidak kita lakukan. Tapi saya ingin mundur -- kita selalu membedah politik kita seolah-olah semuanya tidak terkait. Anda bisa berbicara tentang kebijakan luar negeri, tapi Anda tidak harus menghubungkannya dengan ekonomi.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Every dollar we send abroad to drop a bomb on another kid is a dollar we don't spend here to invest in your kids' school or your health care. The reason that our economy is rigged so that we have a trillionaire and the average person can't afford their basic life is because we have an economy where we've allowed big corporations to buy and sell politicians to rig the system against us. We --
paragraphs.cn: Setiap dolar yang kita kirim ke luar negeri untuk membom anak lain, adalah satu dolar lebih sedikit yang diinvestasikan untuk sekolah anak Anda atau perawatan kesehatan Anda. Ekonomi kita dimanipulasi sehingga kita memiliki triliuner sementara orang biasa tidak mampu membeli kebutuhan dasar, karena kita membiarkan perusahaan besar membeli politisi untuk memanipulasi sistem melawan kita. Kami --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, you're ready to talk economy without being accused of being a communist, or you're basically like, those labels don't matter anymore?
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, apakah Anda siap untuk berbicara tentang ekonomi tanpa dituduh sebagai komunis? Atau pada dasarnya Anda merasa label-label itu tidak lagi penting?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Well, number one, I think people look askance when folks from D.C. call other people communists because we've heard that play run I don't know how many times. But the second point about that is, I just think it's important that we don't center parties in our politics. We center the people who vote for parties and vote in our politics. And I think the problem is that we're having this debate about where the party is going, but we're not actually talking about where people's lives are going.
paragraphs.cn: Yah, pertama, saya pikir ketika orang di Washington menyebut orang lain komunis, orang-orang akan melirik karena kita sudah mendengar trik itu berkali-kali. Tapi tentang ini, poin kedua adalah, saya pikir penting kita tidak berpolitik dengan berpusat pada partai. Kita harus berpusat pada orang yang memilih partai dan berpartisipasi dalam politik. Saya pikir masalahnya adalah kita memperdebatkan ke mana arah partai, tapi sebenarnya tidak membicarakan ke mana arah kehidupan rakyat.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: And the politicians, the politics, that speaks to the lived experience of the average person that actually talks about how do we put money back in your pocket? How do we break the vice grip that corporations have on our politics?
paragraphs.cn: Dan para politisi dan politik itu, jika bisa dekat dengan kehidupan nyata orang biasa, benar-benar membicarakan bagaimana mengembalikan uang ke kantong Anda? Bagaimana mematahkan cengkeraman perusahaan atas politik?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: How do we make sure that you can have health care? That's the kind of politics that's going to win. So, if you don't like where the party is going, then run an alternative that's actually going to address peoples' issues. But so long as you're having this nagging debate about where the party is going from your comfortable home in wherever it is, I just think you're missing the point because too many people are uncomfortable in their homes or don't have a home in the first place.
paragraphs.cn: Bagaimana memastikan Anda bisa mendapatkan perawatan kesehatan? Itulah politik yang bisa menang. Jadi, jika Anda tidak suka arah partai, ajukan alternatif yang benar-benar menyelesaikan masalah rakyat. Tapi selama Anda dari rumah Anda yang nyaman (di mana pun) terus-menerus berdebat tentang arah partai, saya pikir Anda kehilangan intinya, karena terlalu banyak orang yang merasa tidak nyaman di rumah mereka sendiri, atau bahkan tidak punya rumah.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, your opponent, Mallory McMorrow, was on our show last week, as I mentioned. She was criticizing your decision to embrace the support of streamer Hasan Piker to represent your campaign. And what she said was very specific. That she goes, look, I have a problem with him embracing this person in the wake of an attack on a synagogue. This is when a truck was driven into the synagogue. I want to play for you what she said after that. MALLORY MCMORROW (D),
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, lawan Anda, Mallory McMorrow, minggu lalu menjadi tamu kami, seperti yang saya sebutkan. Dia mengkritik keputusan Anda menerima dukungan dari pembawa acara Hasan Piker untuk mewakili kampanye Anda. Dia berkata sangat spesifik. Dia berkata, lihat, setelah serangan di sinagog, saya keberatan dengan Anda menerima orang ini. Saat itu sebuah truk menabrak sinagog. Saya ingin memutar apa yang dia katakan setelahnya. Mallory McMorrow (Demokrat):

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan State Candidate
paragraphs.text: It could have been the largest mass killing of kids in this country's history. And to bring somebody in to represent your campaign, in my mind, is a very different decision than going on somebody's stream and talking to their audience.
paragraphs.cn: Ini mungkin pembantaian anak-anak terbesar dalam sejarah Amerika. Dan mengundang seseorang untuk mewakili kampanye Anda, menurut saya, itu adalah keputusan yang sangat berbeda dengan pergi ke ruang streaming seseorang dan berbicara dengan penonton mereka.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I condemned the Temple Israel attack immediately. Any act of anti-Semitism is wrong and has no place in our politics, no place in our society. But I think what people find frustrating by that perspective is that, when you talk about the mass killing of children, we saw that done by our tax dollars in a genocide in Gaza. And it took, I don't know, two years to talk about that.
paragraphs.cn: Saya segera mengutuk serangan terhadap kuil Israel. Tindakan antisemitisme apa pun salah, tidak punya tempat dalam politik kita, juga tidak punya tempat dalam masyarakat kita. Tapi saya pikir orang frustrasi dengan pandangan ini adalah ketika Anda berbicara tentang pembunuhan massal anak-anak, kita melihat uang pajak kita melakukan hal itu dalam genosida di Gaza. Dan saya tidak tahu butuh dua tahun untuk membicarakan hal ini.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: And I think we have to take seriously the idea that the money that we spent to kill other people's kids there was money that we did not spend to take care of the kids that I was taking care of in Wayne County in Detroit to make sure they had --
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir kita harus serius mempertimbangkan gagasan bahwa uang yang kita habiskan untuk membunuh anak orang lain, seharusnya bisa digunakan untuk merawat anak-anak yang saya rawat di Wayne County, Detroit, memastikan mereka --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, no answer about this embrace of him. Do you think people are making too much or too little about this?
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, tentang menerima dia, tidak ada jawaban. Apakah Anda pikir orang bereaksi berlebihan atau kurang bereaksi terhadap hal ini?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Look, you know, it's a pretty crazy -- you know, so, Hasan Piker was invited to stream from the DNC in 2024. We had a whole debate about whether or not the vice president should have gone on Rogan. The idea that we're going to tie people together and say, well, guilt by association so is just not in keeping with the way most of us think about the world.
paragraphs.cn: Lihat, ini gila -- Anda tahu, Hasan Piker diundang untuk melakukan streaming di Konvensi Nasional Demokrat 2024. Kami pernah memiliki perdebatan besar tentang apakah wakil presiden harus tampil di acara Rogan. Gagasan mengikat orang bersama, mengatakan 'bersalah karena pergaulan', sama sekali tidak sesuai dengan cara kebanyakan dari kita memandang dunia.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I'm sitting on a panel with a very diverse group of people. Did I vet all of you before I came and sat here because one of you might have said something I disagree with? I just think it's a ridiculous way to think about politics.
paragraphs.cn: Saya sekarang duduk bersama kelompok yang sangat beragam untuk berdiskusi. Apakah saya harus memeriksa kalian semua sebelum datang karena mungkin ada di antara kalian yang pernah mengatakan sesuatu yang tidak saya setujui? Saya pikir itu cara berpikir politik yang konyol.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Yes. Well, you're running for office, which is why you're in the hot seat.
paragraphs.cn: Ya. Tapi Anda mencalonkan diri untuk jabatan publik, itulah mengapa Anda menjadi sorotan.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Well, I'm happy to be in the hot seat, but let me just speak to that.
paragraphs.cn: Baiklah, saya dengan senang hati menjadi sorotan, tapi izinkan saya mengatakan beberapa hal tentang ini.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: But this is why you're being vetted. I want to raise something. You used the word "genocide."
paragraphs.cn: Tapi itulah mengapa Anda diperiksa. Saya ingin menyebut satu hal. Anda menggunakan kata 'genosida'.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: This is the kind of thing that people point to over and over again as a problem for a party that is trying to zip up its big tent.
paragraphs.cn: This is exactly the problem that people keep pointing out, and it's a problem for a party trying to expand its inclusiveness.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: "The Detroit Free Press" editorial board just endorsed Mallory McMorrow and said that you are "running a divisive campaign, casting" your "opponents as morally compromised."
paragraphs.cn: The Detroit Free Press editorial board just endorsed Mallory McMorrow and said you're running a 'divisive campaign,' portraying your opponents as 'morally flawed.'

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I think if you've taken money from the people who caused the problems in your life, and then come and tell people, hey, we're going to solve your problems, that's just inconsistent with being serious about solving problems. Now, if that's divisive, I think we need to have hard conversations in our party. I think we need to be talking about who's actually there to solve problems for you.
paragraphs.cn: I think if you take money from people who cause problems in your life and then tell people, 'Hey, we're going to solve your problems,' that's inconsistent with taking the problem seriously. If that's divisive, then I think we need to have a difficult conversation within our party. We need to discuss who is actually solving problems for you.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: If you're not serious about not taking money from the utility company as people's rates are going up, if you're not serious about not taking money from the health insurance corporations who just raised your premiums 15 percent and putting millions of people in bankruptcy, I'm so sorry, I don't think you're serious about solving the problems. If that makes me divisive, that's fine. But look, the American public is going to get to pick. The Michiganders are going to get to pick.
paragraphs.cn: If you don't take seriously not taking money from utility companies when people's electricity bills go up; if you don't take seriously not taking money from health insurance companies when they just raised premiums by 15% and bankrupted millions, then I'm sorry, I don't think you're serious about solving problems. If that makes me divisive, that's fine. But you see, the American public will choose. The people of Michigan will choose.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: And whether we're talking about the parties writ large or we're talking about our Michigan race, it's on people to get to decide.
paragraphs.cn: Whether we're talking about the whole party or our campaign in Michigan, ultimately it's up to the people.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: And this is just what I'm saying. This is not a conversation about parties. So long as we are missing the conversation about everyday people and why they are struggling in their lives, we are missing the point of Democratic politics in the first place.
paragraphs.cn: That's exactly what I'm saying. This isn't a conversation about parties. As long as we lack a conversation about ordinary people and why they struggle in life, we miss the point of democratic politics in the first place.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK, I want you guys -- do you have a question, actually? CHUCK ROCHA, FORMER SENIOR ADVISER TO BERNIE SANDERS' 2016 AND 2020
paragraphs.cn: Okay, I want you to -- -- do you have questions? Chuck Rocha, former senior advisor for Bernie Sanders' 2016 and 2020 campaigns.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: In Michigan, you know, it's a really unique place because of the auto industry and because of an influx of immigrants, lots of Latinos from south Texas, a lot of folks from lots of parts of the country have come in there.
paragraphs.cn: In Michigan, you know, it's a very unique place because of the auto industry and because of the influx of immigrants, many Latinos from South Texas, many people from all over the country came there.

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: As the last working class consultant who never went to college, who worked for the steelworkers union, who have watched my brothers and sisters who grew up there like I did with a single mom trying to figure it out, none of them talked to me every day about the issues that are debated on TV like y'all just finished doing. They're talking to me about things costing too much and not making enough money. Most folks haven't seen real wage gains in years.
paragraphs.cn: As the last working-class advisor who never went to college, worked for the steelworkers union, watched siblings like me raised by single mothers struggle to make a living, none of them talk to me every day about the issues you just debated on TV. They talk to me about things being too expensive and not earning enough. Most people haven't seen real wage growth in years.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Wait, Chuck, is this a question?
paragraphs.cn: Wait, Chuck, is that a question?

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.text: Yes. What are you seeing out there between you and the other candidates and what those people are saying? That's all I care about.
paragraphs.cn: Yes. What do you see out there between you and other candidates, and what are those people saying? I only care about that.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Chuck, thank you for saying that. I'm honored to have earned the UAW endorsement. And look, my dad came to America to build cars. But you talk to autoworkers out in Michigan, Macomb County, in Detroit, and they'll tell you, look, my life has gotten harder to afford every single year for the past ten years. Doesn't matter who's president. Every year I struggle. And they keep telling me that I'm not going to be able to get further. And I'm watching Elon Musk become a trillionaire.
paragraphs.cn: Chuck, thank you for saying that. I'm honored to have the endorsement of the United Auto Workers. You see, my father came to America to build cars. But go talk to auto workers in Michigan, Macomb County, Detroit, and they'll tell you, look, every year for the past decade, my life has become harder to afford. No matter who the president is. Every year I struggle. They keep telling me I can't get ahead. And I watch Elon Musk become a trillionaire.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Do you feel like you would enter a Congress and be able to work in a bipartisan way how -- given how you've spoken about these folks, not just in the party, but across?
paragraphs.cn: Do you think you can work in a bipartisan way once you enter Congress -- -- given the way you talk about these people, not just within the party, but across party lines?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I'll work -- I'll work with anyone who wants to work with me to get things done. But I'll also tell you this, I'm not going to pretend like not calling out the problems that I see is going to make things better.
paragraphs.cn: I will work with anyone willing to work with me to get things done. But I also tell you, I won't pretend that not pointing out the problems I see will make things better.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: The real conversation is not between me and 99 other senators when I get there, it's between me and the 350 million people. It's between all of us and the 350 million people who elect all of us. And that's the mistake that I think both parties are making. We're not having a conversation with the actual public, we're out here debating these issues and we're watching as this discussion is getting further and further away from the lived experience of everyday people.
paragraphs.cn: The real conversation is not between me and the other 99 senators, but between me and 350 million people. It's a conversation between all of us and the 350 million people who elected us. I think that's a mistake both parties are making. We don't talk to the real public; we debate these issues here, watching this discussion drift further and further away from the lived experience of ordinary people.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: One issue I care about a lot on this show is A.I. and its effect on the economy to come. You have talked about public takeover, if I have that right, or privatization.
paragraphs.cn: One issue I care deeply about on this show is artificial intelligence and its impact on the future economy. You've mentioned public ownership, if I understand correctly, or privatization.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Takeover was not the word. If you want to use that word.
paragraphs.cn: Not the word 'takeover.' If you want to use that word.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Oh, what word do you want to use.
paragraphs.cn: Oh, what word would you use?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: A.I. under democracy. I mean democracy is, like, you know, public takeover of government.
paragraphs.cn: Democratic AI. I mean, democracy is, you know, the public taking over the government.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Who would run the company, the government or the people who own the company?
paragraphs.cn: Who runs the company, the government or the owners of the company?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I think that if you have a technology that is going to vastly change the nature of the economy, it's really important that you have democratic oversight of that technology. So, if we call that a takeover, then we've had a takeover of a lot of different things. But I think democracy is a good thing. And A.I. is a scary new technology, while democracy is a very tried and true old technology. And I'm just saying that you should have democracy overseeing the way A.I. is being deployed.
paragraphs.cn: I think if a technology is going to fundamentally change the nature of the economy, then democratic oversight of that technology is very important. So if we call it a takeover, we've already taken over many different things. But I think democracy is good. AI is a scary new technology, and democracy is a time-tested old technology. I'm just saying that democracy should oversee how AI is deployed.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Well, another litmus test then. Would you turn down money from A.I. companies, and do you think other Democrats should?
paragraphs.cn: So, another litmus test. Would you reject donations from AI companies? Do you think other Democrats should do the same?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I turn -- I turn down money from all companies. I don't take corporate money.
paragraphs.cn: I reject -- -- I reject donations from all companies. I don't accept corporate money.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Have you turned down money from an A.I. company?
paragraphs.cn: Have you rejected donations from AI companies?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I've taken -- turned down money from every company. I don't take corporate money.
paragraphs.cn: Saya menolak -- -- menolak sumbangan dari semua perusahaan. Saya tidak menerima dana perusahaan.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: Should Democrats turn down money from A.I. companies?
paragraphs.cn: Haruskah Partai Demokrat menolak sumbangan dari perusahaan AI?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I think Democrats should turn down money from all companies, because you should not be bought by the people who have interests in making sure government works for them instead of working for everyday people. This is a simple thing.
paragraphs.cn: Saya pikir Partai Demokrat harus menolak sumbangan dari semua perusahaan, karena Anda tidak boleh dibeli oleh mereka yang ingin pemerintah melayani mereka, bukan rakyat biasa. Sederhana saja.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: So, you've got a race coming up, August 4th. You going to spend more time back in state, or are you going to be out here talking more to the general voter?
paragraphs.cn: Jadi, akan ada pemilihan pada 4 Agustus. Apakah Anda akan menghabiskan lebih banyak waktu kembali ke negara bagian Anda, atau lebih banyak berbicara di luar kepada pemilih biasa?

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: Well, I'll be out here talking to folks, but I spend almost all my time in Michigan. But since I was here, I figured I'd come and talk to you.
paragraphs.cn: Yah, saya akan berbicara dengan orang-orang di luar, tapi hampir sepanjang waktu saya di Michigan. Tapi karena saya di sini, saya pikir saya bisa datang dan berbicara dengan Anda.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: I appreciate it, because you're an epidemiologist by trade, and y'all had a rough decade. I don't know any public health folks who have come out unscathed.
paragraphs.cn: Saya sangat berterima kasih, karena Anda seorang ahli epidemiologi, dan Anda mengalami masa sulit dalam dekade ini. Tidak ada seorang pun di bidang kesehatan masyarakat yang saya kenal bisa keluar tanpa cedera.

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.text: I would tell you, I think we, as a country, had a rough decade. And we were trying to fix it, address it. And, you know, as an epidemiologist, I'm trained to understand problems as I see them and to try to solve them. So, I hope that I can apply that in our politics as well.
paragraphs.cn: Saya ingin memberi tahu Anda, saya pikir sebagai sebuah negara, kita mengalami masa sulit dalam dekade ini. Kami terus berusaha memperbaikinya, menghadapinya. Dan, sebagai seorang ahli epidemiologi, saya terlatih untuk memahami masalah yang saya lihat dan mencoba menyelesaikannya. Jadi, saya harap saya juga bisa menerapkannya dalam politik kita.

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.text: OK. Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, thank you so much for being here. And thank you all for being in this conversation with us. We know you could be in a lot of places and we're glad you're here with us. I'm Audie Cornish, and the headlines are next. var CNN = CNN || {};
paragraphs.cn: Baik. Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, terima kasih banyak telah datang ke sini. Dan terima kasih kepada semua yang telah berpartisipasi dalam percakapan kami. Kami tahu Anda bisa pergi ke banyak tempat, tapi kami senang Anda di sini. Saya Oti Cornish, dan selanjutnya adalah ringkasan berita.
